stem:
A 4-year-old boy is brought to his GP for a pre-kinder health check. He is otherwise well, growing along the 50th centile, fully immunised, with no exercise intolerance, syncope, cyanosis or breathlessness on play. There is no family history of sudden cardiac death or congenital heart disease, no recent sore throats, no joint pains, and no rashes. On examination he is pink and well perfused with normal femoral pulses, no thrill, a quiet S1 and S2 with no fixed splitting, and a soft musical murmur best heard at the lower left sternal edge while supine.

The murmur has a distinctly vibratory or twangy quality, occupies early to mid-systole, is grade 2/6 in intensity, softens considerably on sitting up, and almost disappears with the Valsalva manoeuvre. There is no diastolic component and no radiation to the neck or axilla.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 96 bpm, RR 20 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, BP 95/58 mmHg right arm
data_table.Examination: Pink, no clubbing or cyanosis, femoral pulses equal to brachials, apex non-displaced, no thrill, no diastolic component, no radiation to neck or axilla
data_table.Growth: Height and weight on 50th centile, head circumference appropriate

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure the family that this is a Still's vibratory innocent murmur and document review at routine health checks; no investigations or paediatric cardiology referral are required
options.rationale: Vibratory grade 1-2 early systolic murmur at the lower left sternal edge in a well 2-7-year-old that softens with Valsalva and on sitting is the classic Still's murmur. Per RCH CPG: Heart murmurs, an asymptomatic child with all innocent-murmur features (no diastolic component, no thrill, soft, varies with posture, normal pulses) does not require ECG, echo or referral.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Heart murmurs in children

options.text: Refer for paediatric cardiology assessment and echocardiogram because innocent murmurs cannot be distinguished from pathological murmurs on examination alone
options.rationale: AU primary-care guidance accepts clinical diagnosis of Still's murmur when all innocent features are present; routine echo is over-investigation. Would be correct only with any red-flag feature (diastolic component, harsh quality, abnormal pulses, failure to thrive) (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Arrange ECG and ASOT today and treat as possible acute rheumatic carditis pending results
options.rationale: No Jones features and no sore throat history; investigating ARF in an asymptomatic child with classic innocent murmur is inappropriate. Would be correct only in high-risk populations with sore throat history and Jones features (Source: RHDAustralia).

options.text: Arrange exercise stress testing to exclude hypertrophic cardiomyopathy before clearing for kindergarten sport
options.rationale: HCM has a harsh systolic murmur that INCREASES with Valsalva (opposite of this), often family history of sudden death. Would be correct only with that profile (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Document the murmur as a small ventricular septal defect and arrange echocardiogram within one month
options.rationale: Small VSD murmur is harsh, pansystolic, often with thrill, and does not soften with Valsalva; the described musical dynamic murmur is innocent. Would be correct only with the pansystolic VSD profile (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Innocent murmurs are common in well children. Still's is the prototypical vibratory early-systolic LLSE murmur of 2 to 7 year olds. Innocent murmurs are soft (grade 1 to 2), systolic only, position-dependent, and accompanied by normal pulses, S2, growth, and no symptoms.
key_points: Innocent murmurs: soft, systolic, sensitive to position, single S2, symptomless, small (grade 1 to 2)
Still's: vibratory LLSE, 2 to 7 years
Venous hum: continuous infraclavicular, abolished supine
Pulmonary flow: ULSE, blowing, often in adolescents
Carey-Coombs: mid-diastolic apical, ARF carditis
explanation.pearls: Red flags warranting echo: diastolic murmur, grade 3 or louder, harsh quality, abnormal S2, symptoms, abnormal pulses, syndromic features, or family history of sudden death.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Heart murmurs in children

stem:
An 8-month-old boy with known unrepaired tetralogy of Fallot presents to a regional ED with his mother. He is awaiting elective surgical repair scheduled in 6 weeks. This morning after a feed he became inconsolable, cried vigorously, and then turned a dusky blue colour with deep, rapid breathing. The episode has been going on for around 10 minutes by the time he reaches the resuscitation cubicle.

He has not vomited, has had no recent fever, and there is no diarrhoea or reduced wet nappies. Baseline SpO2 is usually 88 to 92% per cardiology letters. On arrival he is markedly cyanosed centrally with extremities mottled, breathing deeply at 70 per minute, and the previously loud harsh ejection systolic murmur at the upper left sternal edge is now barely audible. He has good IV access from the ambulance. His weight today is 7.8 kg and he has no known drug allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 deg C, HR 188 bpm, RR 70 /min, SpO2 62% on 15 L/min via non-rebreather, BP 78/46 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Centrally cyanosed, irritable but rousable, deep Kussmaul-like respirations, soft 1/6 ESM ULSE (previously 4/6), CRT 4 seconds peripherally
data_table.Background: Known TOF, awaiting repair, baseline SpO2 88 to 92%, no prior tet spells documented

lead_in: After applying high-flow oxygen, which single next intervention is most appropriate?

options.text: Intravenous adenosine rapid push at the paediatric SVT dose followed by saline flush
options.rationale: Adenosine treats SVT, not a hypercyanotic spell; rhythm is sinus tachycardia from right-to-left shunting. Would be correct in narrow-complex SVT not responding to vagal manoeuvres (Source: RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot).

options.text: Knee-chest positioning with IV morphine slow push
options.rationale: First-line management of a tet spell is knee-chest positioning to raise systemic vascular resistance plus IV or IM morphine 0.1 mg/kg to abolish hyperpnoea and reduce infundibular spasm, as per RCH CPG: Tetralogy of Fallot and AHA scientific statement.

options.text: IV frusemide to offload the right ventricle now
options.rationale: Frusemide reduces preload and worsens right-to-left shunting in a tet spell; volume expansion not depletion is needed. Would never be correct in a tet spell (Source: RCH CPG: ToF).

options.text: Commence prostaglandin E1 infusion peripherally
options.rationale: PGE1 maintains ductal patency in duct-dependent neonatal lesions; at 8 months the duct is closed. Would be correct in a neonate with duct-dependent cyanotic lesion (Source: BMJ Best Practice: ToF).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Tetralogy of Fallot

options.text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate bolus before any other intervention
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is reserved for documented severe acidosis after positioning, oxygen, fluids and morphine have failed. Would be correct only as final step in refractory tet spell with confirmed acidosis (Source: AHA scientific statement).

explanation.summary: A tet spell is a sudden increase in right-to-left shunt through the VSD due to infundibular spasm or a drop in SVR. The murmur softens because pulmonary flow falls. Stepwise management: calm the child, knee-chest position, 100% oxygen, IV fluid bolus 10 to 20 mL/kg, IV or IM morphine 0.1 mg/kg, then IV propranolol 0.1 mg/kg or phenylephrine if refractory.
key_points: Murmur softens during spell (less pulmonary flow)
Knee-chest raises SVR, reducing right-to-left shunt
Morphine reduces hyperpnoea and infundibular spasm
Avoid agents that drop SVR or preload
Refractory: propranolol, phenylephrine, intubation, ECMO, urgent surgery
explanation.pearls: Beta-blockers are used both acutely and as a bridge to surgery to prevent spells; never give inotropes that increase contractility (worsens infundibular spasm).

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Tetralogy of Fallot

stem:
A 10-week-old term male infant is brought to a paediatric clinic by his parents because of poor feeding and slow weight gain. They report that he sweats profusely during breastfeeds, often pauses to catch his breath after a few sucks, and takes more than 40 minutes per feed. He has dropped from the 50th to the 9th weight centile over 6 weeks.

There has been no cyanosis, no syncope, and no fevers. At his newborn baby check a soft murmur had been noted and an echocardiogram at 4 weeks showed a moderately large perimembranous ventricular septal defect with left-to-right shunt and mild left atrial and ventricular dilatation. He is now visibly tachypnoeic at rest, with intercostal recession, a hyperdynamic precordium, a 3/6 harsh pansystolic murmur at the lower left sternal edge, and a palpable liver edge 3 cm below the costal margin.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, HR 170 bpm, RR 64 /min, SpO2 97% on room air, BP 82/48 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Tachypnoeic with subcostal and intercostal recession, hyperdynamic precordium, 3/6 PSM LLSE with thrill, hepatomegaly 3 cm, mild bibasal crackles
data_table.Growth: Weight dropped from 50th to 9th centile since birth
data_table.Echo (4 weeks): Moderate perimembranous VSD, left-to-right shunt, mild LA and LV dilatation, normal LV function

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate initial medical management?

options.text: Reassure parents and review in 3 months expecting spontaneous closure
options.rationale: Spontaneous closure is common only for small muscular VSDs; failure to thrive and heart failure demand treatment. Would be correct only for a small muscular VSD without haemodynamic features (Source: RCH CPG: Heart Failure in Children).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Heart failure in children

options.text: Commence oral propranolol three times daily and high-energy formula
options.rationale: Beta-blockers are not first-line in infant left-to-right shunt failure; diuretic plus afterload reduction is standard. Would be correct in tet-spell prophylaxis or other specific indications (Source: BMJ Best Practice: VSD).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Ventricular septal defect

options.text: Commence oral frusemide BD and an ACE inhibitor with high-energy feeds
options.rationale: Standard medical therapy of large VSD with heart failure in infancy is loop diuretic plus ACE inhibitor for afterload reduction plus calorie-dense feeds, with surgical closure typically before 6 to 12 months if failure to thrive or pulmonary hypertension persists, per RCH CPG and BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Heart failure in children
BMJ Best Practice: Ventricular septal defect

options.text: Commence prostaglandin E1 infusion to maintain ductal patency
options.rationale: PGE1 is for duct-dependent lesions; a VSD with left-to-right shunt does not depend on the duct. Would be correct in duct-dependent cyanotic lesions (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Ventricular septal defect

options.text: Commence oxygen therapy at 2 L/min via nasal prongs for resting tachypnoea
options.rationale: Supplemental oxygen lowers PVR and worsens pulmonary overcirculation in a VSD; this is the textbook iatrogenic error in left-to-right shunts. Would be correct only for documented hypoxaemia from another cause (Source: RCH CPG: Heart Failure).
source_refs: RCH CPG: Heart failure in children

explanation.summary: Symptomatic large VSD presents at 6 to 12 weeks as pulmonary vascular resistance falls and left-to-right shunt increases. Features include tachypnoea, sweating with feeds, hepatomegaly and failure to thrive. Medical management is loop diuretic plus ACE inhibitor plus high-energy feeds, with surgical closure for ongoing failure to thrive, pulmonary hypertension, or large unrestricted shunt before fixed pulmonary vascular disease develops.
key_points: Murmur is harsh pansystolic LLSE, often with thrill
Frusemide 1 to 2 mg/kg BD plus ACE inhibitor (captopril or enalapril)
Avoid supplemental O2 (drops PVR, worsens shunt)
Surgery before fixed pulmonary vascular disease
Small muscular VSDs often close spontaneously
explanation.pearls: Symptoms typically appear at 4 to 8 weeks because pulmonary vascular resistance must fall before left-to-right shunting becomes haemodynamically significant.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Heart failure in children
BMJ Best Practice: Ventricular septal defect

stem:
A preterm male infant born at 27 weeks gestation is now day 6 of life on the neonatal intensive care unit. Birthweight was 950 g. He was initially ventilated for surfactant-deficient lung disease and is currently on synchronised intermittent mandatory ventilation with FiO2 0.35 and mean airway pressure 9 cm H2O. Over the past 24 hours he has required escalating ventilatory support, has developed widening pulse pressure, bounding femoral pulses, and a continuous murmur at the upper left sternal edge.

He has been passing only 0.8 mL/kg/h of urine despite normal fluid intake. A formal echocardiogram performed this morning confirms a haemodynamically significant patent ductus arteriosus with a 2.4 mm internal diameter, left-to-right shunt, left atrium to aortic root ratio of 1.8, and reversed end-diastolic flow in the post-ductal descending aorta. Renal function and platelet count are within accepted neonatal limits and there is no evidence of necrotising enterocolitis or active intraventricular haemorrhage.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 168 bpm, RR per vent 50, SpO2 92% on FiO2 0.35, BP 48/22 mmHg (wide pulse pressure), MAP 31 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Bounding peripheral pulses, hyperdynamic precordium, continuous machinery murmur ULSE, lungs with diffuse crackles
data_table.Echo: PDA 2.4 mm, LA:Ao 1.8, reversed end-diastolic flow descending aorta, no other structural disease
data_table.Bloods: Platelets 220 x10^9/L, creatinine 55 micromol/L, urine output 0.8 mL/kg/h

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence prostaglandin E1 infusion to maintain ductal patency
options.rationale: PGE1 keeps the duct open in duct-dependent lesions; here a haemodynamically significant PDA needs closure, not patency. Would be correct only for duct-dependent neonatal cyanotic lesions (Source: RCH NICU PDA Guideline).
source_refs: RCH NICU CPG: Patent ductus arteriosus

options.text: Restrict fluids to a conservative ml/kg/day target and continue conservative management
options.rationale: Conservative is reasonable for non-haemodynamically significant PDA; this infant has clear significance (escalating ventilation, wide pulse pressure, reversed diastolic flow). Would be correct only for small non-significant PDAs (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Patent ductus arteriosus in preterm neonates

options.text: Refer for surgical PDA ligation now as first-line therapy
options.rationale: Surgical ligation is reserved for failure or contraindication of medical therapy; pharmacological closure is first-line. Would be correct after failed COX-inhibitor course or contraindication (Source: RCH NICU CPG: PDA).
source_refs: RCH NICU CPG: Patent ductus arteriosus

options.text: Course of ibuprofen or indomethacin for pharmacological PDA closure
options.rationale: In preterm infants with haemodynamically significant PDA and no contraindication (NEC, active IVH, renal failure, thrombocytopenia), a course of ibuprofen or indomethacin is first-line pharmacological closure, per RCH NICU CPG: PDA.
source_refs: RCH NICU CPG: Patent ductus arteriosus
BMJ Best Practice: Patent ductus arteriosus in preterm neonates

options.text: Commence oral paracetamol six-hourly indefinitely as definitive closure agent
options.rationale: Paracetamol is an emerging second-line as a 3-7 day course when COX inhibitors are contraindicated, not indefinite or first-line. Would be correct as a short course with contraindication to indomethacin/ibuprofen (Source: BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Patent ductus arteriosus in preterm neonates

explanation.summary: Haemodynamically significant PDA in preterm neonates causes pulmonary overcirculation, prolonged ventilation, IVH, NEC and BPD. Echo criteria for significance include diameter > 1.5 mm, LA:Ao > 1.4, reversed diastolic flow in descending aorta. First-line closure is a COX-inhibitor course (ibuprofen or indomethacin) or paracetamol if contraindicated, with surgical ligation if pharmacological therapy fails. Term PDA usually closes spontaneously within 72 hours; persistent term PDA is managed by elective device closure beyond infancy.
key_points: Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg then 5 mg/kg at 24 and 48 h is now preferred (less renal toxicity than indomethacin)
Contraindications: NEC, IVH, oliguria, thrombocytopenia, sepsis
Surgical ligation if medical fails and PDA remains significant
Term infants: catheter device closure beyond infancy
Echo confirms significance before treatment
explanation.pearls: Indomethacin reduces IVH rates but at the cost of more transient oliguria; ibuprofen has become the more widely used agent in Australian NICUs.

sources.label: RCH NICU CPG: Patent ductus arteriosus
BMJ Best Practice: Patent ductus arteriosus in preterm neonates

stem:
A 6-day-old term male infant is brought to a regional ED by his mother. He was born by uncomplicated normal vaginal delivery at 39+2 weeks with birthweight 3.4 kg, passed all routine newborn checks, and was discharged at 36 hours of life. Over the past 24 hours he has fed poorly, has been increasingly lethargic, and his mother noticed his legs looking mottled and feeling cool while his upper body remained warm.

He has had no fevers and no vomiting. On arrival he is pale, lethargic, and grunting. Brachial pulses are easily palpable bilaterally, but the femoral pulses are absent. Pre-ductal SpO2 on the right hand is 96%, while post-ductal SpO2 on the right foot is 84%. Blood pressure in the right arm is 78/44 mmHg and in the right leg is 42/28 mmHg, giving a clear upper-to-lower limb gradient. The liver is palpable 3 cm below the costal margin and there is metabolic acidosis on the venous blood gas.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.4 deg C, HR 180 bpm, RR 70 /min, BP arm 78/44 / leg 42/28 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Absent femoral pulses, brachial pulses present, mottled cool legs, warm upper body, hepatomegaly 3 cm, single loud S2
data_table.VBG: pH 7.18, lactate 6.8 mmol/L, HCO3 12 mmol/L, glucose 4.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management while awaiting transfer?

options.text: Intravenous frusemide and conservative fluid restriction
options.rationale: Diuresis is adjunctive for pulmonary congestion but does not address lethal ductal closure in duct-dependent systemic circulation. Would be correct only as adjunct once PGE1 has restored ductal flow (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Cardiology).

options.text: Intramuscular vitamin K 1 mg and oral feeds withheld pending surgical review
options.rationale: Vitamin K does not treat duct closure; PGE1 is required to reopen the duct. Would be correct as routine post-birth, not as immediate management of duct-dependent shock (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Coarctation).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Coarctation of the aorta

options.text: High-flow nasal cannula oxygen titrated to SpO2 above 95%
options.rationale: Supplemental O2 further constricts the ductus and accelerates collapse in duct-dependent systemic circulation; target SpO2 is 85-95 percent, not over 95. Would be correct only with confirmed non-cardiac hypoxaemia (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Intravenous adenosine rapid push to terminate suspected supraventricular tachycardia
options.rationale: No narrow-complex tachycardia described; differential pulses and saturation differential are diagnostic of coarctation. Would be correct in SVT (Source: RCH CPG: SVT).

options.text: Commence prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil) infusion via secure IV access
options.rationale: Duct-dependent systemic circulation from coarctation or interrupted aortic arch presents at day 5 to 10 with shock as the duct closes; PGE1 at 0.01 to 0.05 microg/kg/min reopens the duct and is the immediate life-saving therapy, per RCH CPG: Neonatal cardiology and BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Coarctation of the aorta

explanation.summary: Critical coarctation presents at the end of the first week as the duct closes, producing shock, absent femoral pulses, upper-to-lower limb BP gradient, pre/post-ductal SpO2 differential, and metabolic acidosis. Immediate management is PGE1 infusion (commonly 0.01 to 0.05 microg/kg/min titrated), correction of acidosis, careful fluid resuscitation, and urgent transfer for surgical repair. Side effects of PGE1 include apnoea, fever, hypotension, and flushing; be prepared to intubate.
key_points: Duct-dependent systemic: coarctation, interrupted arch, critical AS, HLHS
PGE1 0.01 to 0.05 microg/kg/min IV
Apnoea is a common side effect; have airway ready
Avoid hyperoxia (constricts duct)
Definitive: surgical or catheter repair
explanation.pearls: If the duct closes before diagnosis, the infant can deteriorate from well to moribund within hours; any unwell neonate in the first 2 weeks needs femoral pulses and 4-limb BPs.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Coarctation of the aorta

stem:
A 5-month-old previously well girl is brought to a metropolitan ED by ambulance because of poor feeding and pallor for 3 hours. Her mother reports that she felt the baby's chest racing earlier this morning and noticed she was breathing fast and looking sweaty during a feed. There has been no fever, no diarrhoea, and no preceding viral illness over the past week.

On arrival to resus she is pale, mottled, and lethargic but responsive to voice. Femoral and brachial pulses are present but extremely rapid and impossible to count manually. A 12-lead ECG shows a regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 285 bpm with no clearly visible P waves and a constant RR interval. There are no delta waves on the resting trace once an experienced cardiologist reviews her baseline ECG from a previous well-child check. She has good peripheral IV access placed by paramedics en route. She is not yet hypotensive and remains alert.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 285 bpm (ECG), RR 60 /min, SpO2 96% on room air, BP 76/48 mmHg
data_table.ECG: Regular narrow-complex tachycardia, rate 285, no visible P waves, constant RR, no QRS widening
data_table.Examination: Pale, mottled, responsive, CRT 3 seconds, no hepatomegaly yet, lungs clear

lead_in: After applying continuous cardiac monitoring and defibrillation pads, what is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Crushed ice to the face for 30 seconds
options.rationale: In a haemodynamically stable infant with SVT, the diving reflex provoked by an ice bag to the face is the recommended vagal manoeuvre and can be attempted first while preparing adenosine, per RCH CPG: SVT and ANZCOR guideline 12.6.
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 12.6: Resuscitation in paediatric tachyarrhythmias

options.text: Immediate unsynchronised defibrillation now
options.rationale: Unsynchronised defibrillation is for VF/pulseless VT; unstable SVT needs synchronised cardioversion at 0.5-1 J/kg. Would be correct in pulseless VT or VF (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 12.6).
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 12.6: Resuscitation in paediatric tachyarrhythmias

options.text: Intravenous verapamil by slow IV push
options.rationale: Verapamil is contraindicated under 12 months (risk of refractory hypotension and cardiac arrest). Would be correct only in older children with stable SVT and no Wolff-Parkinson-White pre-excitation (Source: AMH Children's Dosing Companion).

options.text: Oral propranolol as a stat dose
options.rationale: Oral beta-blockade is for chronic prevention, not acute termination in an unwell infant. Would be correct only as maintenance after acute conversion (Source: RCH CPG: SVT).

options.text: Right carotid sinus massage for 10 seconds
options.rationale: Carotid sinus massage is not used in infants; ice to the face is the recommended vagal manoeuvre at this age. Would be correct only in older children/adolescents and adults (Source: ANZCOR 12.6).
source_refs: ANZCOR Guideline 12.6: Resuscitation in paediatric tachyarrhythmias

explanation.summary: Infant SVT typically presents as poor feeding, pallor and tachypnoea with rates over 220 bpm and a narrow complex on ECG. In the stable infant: vagal manoeuvre (ice to face) then IV adenosine 0.1 mg/kg, repeat 0.2 mg/kg, then 0.3 mg/kg up to a single max of 12 mg, given rapidly into a large vein with saline flush. In the unstable infant proceed straight to synchronised DC cardioversion 0.5 to 1 J/kg, then 2 J/kg.
key_points: Stable: ice to face, then adenosine 0.1 then 0.2 mg/kg
Unstable: synchronised cardioversion 0.5 to 1 J/kg
Avoid verapamil under 12 months
Maintenance with propranolol or flecainide guided by cardiology
Check for WPW on sinus ECG after termination
explanation.pearls: Adenosine causes a brief, alarming asystolic pause; counsel the team, have monitor/defib pads on, and use a 3-way tap so the saline flush immediately follows the bolus.

sources.label: ANZCOR Guideline 12.6: Resuscitation in paediatric tachyarrhythmias

stem:
A 15-year-old girl is referred to a paediatric cardiology outpatient clinic after two episodes of exertional syncope. The first occurred while sprinting at school athletics, with a brief loss of consciousness during which her friends say her limbs jerked for around 10 seconds before she came round confused for 30 seconds. The second was while jumping in a pool 6 weeks later.

There is no history of typical post-ictal phase, tongue biting, or incontinence. A maternal uncle died suddenly in his 20s while swimming; the family was told it was drowning. She takes no regular medications and has never used a QT-prolonging drug. Examination is unremarkable with normal heart sounds, no murmur, and normal blood pressure. A resting 12-lead ECG at the clinic is shown to a paediatric electrophysiologist.

Manual measurement and Bazett correction across multiple leads in the same patient give a QTc of 510 ms, with broad-based notched T-waves in the precordial leads.

data_table.ECG: Sinus rhythm 72 bpm, QTc 510 ms (Bazett), broad-based notched T waves V2 to V4, no delta wave
data_table.Family history: Maternal uncle sudden death in 20s while swimming, no other known cardiac disease
data_table.Triggers: Both episodes occurred during exertion (sprint, swimming)

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassurance, electrolyte replacement, and follow-up ECG in 12 months
options.rationale: QTc over 480 ms with exertional syncope and family history meets LQTS diagnostic criteria; reassurance is the missed-LQTS anti-pattern. Would be correct only with borderline QTc and no syncope/family history (Source: Heart Rhythm Society/AHA expert consensus).
source_refs: AHA/HRS expert consensus on inherited primary arrhythmia syndromes

options.text: Non-selective beta-blocker propranolol, exercise restriction, cascade ECG screening of relatives
options.rationale: LQTS with syncope is high-risk; non-selective beta-blockers (propranolol or nadolol) reduce events, exercise restriction is recommended particularly for swimming in LQT1, and first-degree relatives need cascade screening with ECG +/- genetic testing, per AHA/HRS consensus and BMJ Best Practice.
source_refs: AHA/HRS expert consensus on inherited primary arrhythmia syndromes
BMJ Best Practice: Long QT syndrome

options.text: Commence sotalol 80 mg twice daily for QT shortening
options.rationale: Sotalol prolongs QT and is contraindicated in LQTS; non-QT-prolonging beta-blockers (propranolol, nadolol) are preferred. Would never be correct in LQTS (Source: AMH; BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Long QT syndrome

options.text: Immediate implantable cardioverter defibrillator (ICD) implantation as first-line therapy
options.rationale: ICD is reserved for arrest survivors or breakthrough syncope on beta-blocker; first-line is beta-blocker plus lifestyle modification. Would be correct after beta-blocker failure (Source: AHA/HRS consensus).
source_refs: AHA/HRS expert consensus on inherited primary arrhythmia syndromes

options.text: Commence levetiracetam after referral to a neurologist for presumed seizures
options.rationale: Exertional trigger, family sudden death, and prolonged QTc point to LQTS not epilepsy; hypoxic limb jerking mimics seizure but is not seizure. Would be correct only with confirmed seizure on EEG (Source: BMJ Best Practice: LQTS).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Long QT syndrome

explanation.summary: Diagnostic QTc cut-offs: a single resting QTc > 480 ms with unexplained syncope, or > 500 ms without symptoms, supports LQTS (Schwartz score also used). Management includes activity modification (especially swimming/competitive sport in LQT1), avoidance of QT-prolonging drugs and electrolyte derangement, non-selective beta-blockade (propranolol or nadolol), genetic testing, cascade family screening, and ICD for survivors of cardiac arrest or breakthrough events.
key_points: QTc > 480 ms with syncope or > 500 ms asymptomatic suggests LQTS
LQT1: exertion (swimming); LQT2: auditory/emotion; LQT3: rest/sleep
First-line: nadolol or propranolol
Avoid sotalol and other QT-prolonging drugs
Cascade screening of first-degree relatives
explanation.pearls: A sudden death in a young relative while swimming is classic for LQT1; warn the family about both medication and electrolyte triggers and provide a written drug-avoidance list.

sources.label: AHA/HRS expert consensus on inherited primary arrhythmia syndromes
BMJ Best Practice: Long QT syndrome

stem:
A 9-year-old Aboriginal girl from a remote community in the Northern Territory is reviewed at a regional hospital after 10 days of migratory polyarthritis affecting her knees, ankles, and wrists in sequence over the preceding week. Each joint has been hot, painful, and swollen for 2 to 3 days before migrating to another joint, with dramatic and rapid resolution. She also had a sore throat 4 weeks ago that resolved without antibiotic treatment, and a throat swab taken at that time was not collected.

She has no prior diagnosis of acute rheumatic fever and no known rheumatic heart disease on previous school-based screening echocardiograms performed at age 7. She has not had a documented fever during her current presentation, though her mother reports a febrile illness during the recent pharyngitis. There is no carditis clinically and a transthoracic echocardiogram performed today shows a structurally normal heart with no valvular regurgitation or pericardial effusion.

ECG demonstrates sinus rhythm with a PR interval of 220 ms (above the age-related upper limit). Anti-streptolysin O titre is 540 IU/mL and anti-DNase B is 380 U/mL, both elevated above the laboratory reference range. C-reactive protein is 60 mg/L, ESR is 65 mm/h, and a full blood count is otherwise unremarkable. The treating team is applying the 2020 RHDAustralia guideline using the criteria for high-risk populations.

data_table.Clinical: Migratory polyarthritis (knees, ankles, wrists), no carditis, no chorea, no subcutaneous nodules, no erythema marginatum, afebrile
data_table.Investigations: ASOT 540 IU/mL, anti-DNase B 380 U/mL, CRP 60 mg/L, ESR 65 mm/h, ECG PR 220 ms (above age threshold), echo normal
data_table.Background: Remote NT community, high-risk population, no prior ARF or RHD, sore throat 4 weeks ago

lead_in: Which single statement most accurately reflects whether this presentation meets the 2020 RHDAustralia Jones criteria for an initial episode of acute rheumatic fever in a high-risk population?

options.text: Does not meet criteria because she is afebrile and fever is mandatory for diagnosis
options.rationale: Fever is a minor criterion, not mandatory; diagnosis requires 2 major or 1 major + 2 minor with GAS evidence. Would be correct only if fever were a stated mandatory feature (it is not) (Source: RHDAustralia 2020 Jones).

options.text: Does not meet criteria because echocardiogram is normal and subclinical carditis is required
options.rationale: Carditis is one of several majors, not mandatory; absence does not exclude ARF if other criteria met. Would never be correct as a rule-out (Source: RHDAustralia 2020).

options.text: Meets definite initial ARF: polyarthritis (major in high-risk), prolonged PR and elevated APRs (minors), plus recent GAS
options.rationale: Under the 2020 RHDAustralia high-risk criteria, polyarthritis is a major; she has 1 major (polyarthritis) plus 3 minors (prolonged PR for age, raised CRP and ESR) plus serological evidence of recent GAS (raised ASOT and anti-DNase B), satisfying the 1 major + 2 minor + GAS evidence threshold for definite initial ARF, per RHDAustralia 2020.

options.text: Meets criteria only as probable ARF because monoarthritis is required in high-risk populations
options.rationale: In high-risk populations monoarthritis/polyarthralgia can also be a major; polyarthritis remains a major giving definite (not probable) ARF when criteria met. Would never be correct (Source: RHDAustralia 2020).

options.text: Does not meet criteria because anti-DNase B is non-specific and only ASOT counts as GAS evidence
options.rationale: Anti-DNase B is accepted GAS evidence in addition to ASOT; the criteria are met. Would never be correct (Source: RHDAustralia 2020).

explanation.summary: Acute rheumatic fever and rheumatic heart disease cause one of the largest health gaps for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Australians, with incidence rates 100-fold higher than non-Indigenous populations. Diagnosis uses Jones criteria modified for high-risk populations: 2 majors, or 1 major plus 2 minors, plus evidence of preceding group A strep infection (ASOT, anti-DNase B, throat swab or PCR). Majors in high-risk populations include carditis (clinical or subclinical), polyarthritis, monoarthritis or polyarthralgia, chorea, erythema marginatum and subcutaneous nodules; minors include fever above 38, ESR above 30 or CRP above 30, prolonged PR interval (age-adjusted), and monoarthralgia. All confirmed cases require notification to the state RHD register, echocardiogram for baseline carditis assessment, and benzathine penicillin G secondary prophylaxis 4-weekly for a minimum of 10 years or until age 21 (longer for those with carditis or valve disease).
key_points: High-risk Australia: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander, Maori/Pacific Islander, immigrants from endemic areas
Majors high-risk: carditis (incl. subclinical), polyarthritis OR monoarthritis OR polyarthralgia, chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules
Minors high-risk: fever >= 38 deg C, ESR >= 30 mm/h or CRP elevated, prolonged PR for age, monoarthralgia
Recurrence: 2 majors, OR 1 major + 2 minors, OR 3 minors + GAS evidence
Treat: benzathine penicillin G IM and secondary prophylaxis
explanation.pearls: In high-risk populations (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander, Maori, Pacific Islander), the 2020 RHDAustralia Jones criteria accept monoarthritis and polyarthralgia as major manifestations and lower the minor thresholds for fever (38) and ESR (30); secondary prophylaxis with benzathine penicillin G 4-weekly for 10+ years is the cornerstone (RHDAustralia 2020).

stem:
A 5-month-old male infant is referred to a paediatric ED with 6 days of unexplained fever to 39.5 deg C unresponsive to paracetamol. His mother reports increasing irritability and one day of red, cracked lips. He has had no diarrhoea, no cough, and no contact with sick household members. He has been investigated extensively in the community over 3 days.

Urinalysis is sterile on culture. Blood cultures at 48 hours are negative, and a full septic screen including CSF is unremarkable. He has had no rash and no peripheral changes apart from puffy hands noted today. He has bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection but no lymphadenopathy is palpable. C-reactive protein is 90 mg/L, ESR 70 mm/h, platelets 580 x10^9/L, ALT 65 U/L, albumin 28 g/L, white cell count 17 x10^9/L with neutrophil predominance, and urine microscopy shows 25 white cells per high-power field without organisms.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.4 deg C, HR 175 bpm, RR 42 /min, BP 92/50 mmHg
data_table.Clinical features: Bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, red cracked lips, puffy hands, no rash, no lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: CRP 90 mg/L, ESR 70 mm/h, platelets 580 x10^9/L, WCC 17 with neutrophilia, ALT 65, albumin 28, sterile pyuria (25 WBC/hpf, no organisms)
data_table.Cultures: Blood, urine, CSF all negative at 48 h

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home on paracetamol and ibuprofen for presumed viral illness, review 48 hours
options.rationale: Persistent fever over 5 days with KD features in an infant under 6 months mandates urgent assessment for coronary artery disease, not discharge. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki).

options.text: Commence IV ceftriaxone for occult bacterial sepsis and continue cultures
options.rationale: Persistently negative cultures and no antibiotic response shift the differential to Kawasaki; continued empirical antibiotics delay IVIG. Would be correct only with culture-positive infection (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Kawasaki).

options.text: Request transthoracic echocardiogram to look for coronary artery aneurysms before any further treatment decisions
options.rationale: Echo is essential and arranged urgently, but treatment with IVIG must not be delayed pending echo in incomplete KD with supportive labs. Would be correct only if echo could be done immediately at no delay to IVIG (Source: AHA Kawasaki).

options.text: Treat empirically with IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin and arrange echocardiogram
options.rationale: Infants under 6 months commonly present with incomplete KD. Fever >= 5 days plus 2 to 3 principal features plus supportive labs (CRP >= 30 mg/L or ESR >= 40 mm/h, plus 3 or more of anaemia, platelets >= 450, albumin <= 30, raised ALT, WCC >= 15, sterile pyuria) supports incomplete KD; treatment is IVIG 2 g/kg single infusion plus aspirin, with urgent echo to assess coronaries, per AHA statement and RCH CPG.

options.text: Commence high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone as monotherapy without IVIG
options.rationale: Steroids are an adjunct (high-risk patients, IVIG-resistant disease), not monotherapy; IVIG remains first-line. Would be correct only as adjunct alongside IVIG in high-risk patients (Source: AHA Kawasaki).

explanation.summary: Classic KD requires fever >= 5 days plus 4 of 5 principal features (conjunctival injection, oral changes, polymorphous rash, extremity changes, cervical lymphadenopathy > 1.5 cm). Infants under 6 months and over 8 years commonly have incomplete disease with fewer features but a high risk of coronary artery aneurysms. The AHA algorithm allows diagnosis when fever >= 5 days plus 2 to 3 features plus supportive labs are present, or with abnormal echocardiogram. Treatment: IVIG 2 g/kg single infusion plus aspirin (high-dose anti-inflammatory then low-dose antiplatelet).
key_points: Incomplete KD: think in any infant under 6 months with fever > 7 days
Supportive labs: CRP >= 30, ESR >= 40, plus 3 of (anaemia, plt >= 450 after day 7, albumin <= 30, raised ALT, WCC >= 15, sterile pyuria)
IVIG 2 g/kg single infusion within 10 days reduces aneurysm risk from 25% to under 5%
Aspirin (high-dose anti-inflammatory, then low-dose antiplatelet)
Echo at diagnosis, 2 weeks and 6 to 8 weeks
explanation.pearls: Sterile pyuria is a classic and often missed clue; always check a urine in prolonged unexplained fever.

stem:
A 36-hour-old term male newborn is reviewed on the postnatal ward at a metropolitan tertiary hospital. His mother is 39 years old and declined non-invasive prenatal testing and combined first-trimester screening during pregnancy; the antenatal morphology scan at 20 weeks was reported as normal with limited views of the cardiac four-chamber view. Birthweight was 3.1 kg at 39+1 weeks gestation.

On clinical examination the baby has marked generalised hypotonia, brachycephaly with flattened occiput, upslanting palpebral fissures with bilateral epicanthic folds, small low-set ears with overfolded helices, a flat nasal bridge, a protruding tongue, a single palmar crease bilaterally, and a wide gap between the first and second toes. The midwife noted bilious green vomiting after the first three feeds and the abdomen is now distended with absent bowel sounds.

A plain abdominal radiograph performed at 24 hours of life shows a classic double bubble appearance with no distal gas. There is a soft 2/6 systolic murmur at the lower left sternal edge with no thrill. Femoral pulses are easily palpable and equal bilaterally. He is not cyanotic and pre/post-ductal saturations are both 96%. Full blood count taken on capillary sample shows a white cell count of 24 x10^9/L with 35% blasts on the film, although the consultant haematologist notes the morphology and clinical context are most consistent with transient abnormal myelopoiesis. The neonatal team needs to prioritise the most urgent investigation in the first 24 hours.

data_table.Dysmorphology: Hypotonia, brachycephaly, upslanting palpebral fissures, epicanthic folds, single palmar creases, sandal-gap toes
data_table.GI: Bilious vomiting, double-bubble on AXR, no distal gas
data_table.Cardiac: Soft 2/6 LLSE systolic murmur, femoral pulses present, no cyanosis
data_table.Haematology: WCC 24 x10^9/L, 35% blasts, morphology in keeping with TAM
data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 158, RR 50, SpO2 96% on room air

lead_in: Which single investigation is the highest priority in the newborn screening period for this infant?

options.text: Lumbar puncture and CSF cytology to exclude CNS involvement of leukaemia
options.rationale: Transient abnormal myelopoiesis usually self-resolves and does not require LP at presentation; cardiac and surgical assessment take priority. Would be correct only with persistent blast cells and CNS signs (Source: RCH CPG: Down Syndrome).

options.text: MRI brain to characterise dysmorphology and exclude structural CNS malformation
options.rationale: Routine MRI brain is not part of newborn Down syndrome assessment. Would be correct only with focal neuro signs or seizures (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Hip ultrasound to screen for developmental dysplasia of the hip
options.rationale: Hip US is performed at 6 weeks (or earlier with clinical signs); not a 24-hour priority over cardiac/surgical assessment. Would be correct at scheduled 6-week time-point (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: TSH and free T4 measurement to detect congenital hypothyroidism
options.rationale: Congenital hypothyroidism is screened on the newborn bloodspot at 48-72 hours; important but lower priority than urgent cardiac assessment with duodenal atresia. Would be correct as planned screen, not the highest priority now (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Transthoracic echocardiogram to define cardiac anatomy before surgical correction of duodenal atresia
options.rationale: Up to 50% of T21 newborns have a structural cardiac defect, commonly AVSD. The infant requires urgent laparotomy for duodenal atresia; defining cardiac anatomy by echo is essential before anaesthesia to anticipate haemodynamic risks and plan management, per RCH CPG: Down syndrome and BMJ Best Practice.

explanation.summary: All newborns with suspected or confirmed T21 require echocardiography within the first month (within days if a duct-dependent lesion is suspected or surgery is planned), karyotype confirmation, audiology, ophthalmology referral, thyroid screening on bloodspot, and FBC. With concurrent duodenal atresia requiring laparotomy and a murmur, urgent pre-operative echo is essential to characterise an AVSD or other lesion.
key_points: Up to 50% have CHD; AVSD is the commonest serious defect
Duodenal atresia in 5% of T21 newborns
TAM in 10%; usually self-resolves but 20% develop AML by age 5
Newborn workup: echo, karyotype, FBC, TSH, audiology, red reflex, hip US at 6 weeks
Lifelong: regular thyroid, coeliac, vision, hearing, OSA screening
explanation.pearls: Before anaesthesia in any T21 infant, you must know the cardiac anatomy and the C1-C2 stability for older children (atlanto-axial instability).

stem:
A 2-day-old term female neonate is reviewed on the postnatal ward after the midwife noted persistent bilateral lower limb swelling and unusual neck skin folds. Birthweight was 2.8 kg at 39 weeks; the pregnancy was complicated by increased nuchal translucency at 12 weeks, but the parents declined further prenatal testing. On examination she has marked pedal and dorsal hand lymphoedema, a webbed posterior neck with redundant skin folds, a broad shield chest with widely spaced nipples, and a low posterior hairline.

Right brachial pulse is normal but both femoral pulses feel weaker than the brachials and there is a 22 mmHg systolic differential between right arm and right leg cuff blood pressures. She is feeding well and not cyanosed. SpO2 pre-ductal is 97% and post-ductal is 95%. A genetic karyotype has been sent and is pending. The team must decide which finding warrants the most urgent investigation now.

data_table.Dysmorphology: Pedal lymphoedema, webbed neck, broad shield chest, widely spaced nipples, low posterior hairline
data_table.Cardiovascular: Right brachial pulse normal, both femoral pulses weaker, right arm BP 78/46 vs right leg 56/30 mmHg (22 mmHg systolic gradient)
data_table.Vitals: T 36.7, HR 148, RR 46, SpO2 97 pre-ductal / 95 post-ductal

lead_in: Which single feature in this newborn most urgently warrants an immediate echocardiogram?

options.text: Weakened femoral pulses with a right arm-leg BP differential suggesting coarctation
options.rationale: Approximately 30% of Turner syndrome neonates have congenital heart disease, of which coarctation and bicuspid aortic valve are commonest. Weak femorals and an upper-to-lower limb BP gradient are clinical signs of coarctation that mandate urgent echo to assess for duct-dependent systemic circulation, per RCH CPG: Turner syndrome and BMJ Best Practice.

options.text: Pedal lymphoedema, which suggests a renovascular cause requiring urgent renal artery Doppler
options.rationale: Lymphoedema is due to congenital lymphatic dysplasia and does not require renal artery imaging; cardiac assessment is the priority. Would never be correct (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Turner Syndrome).

options.text: Webbed neck, which mandates urgent cervical spine MRI for atlanto-axial instability
options.rationale: Atlanto-axial instability is associated with Down syndrome, not Turner; webbed neck reflects cystic hygroma resolution. Would be correct in Down syndrome with neck signs (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Widely spaced nipples, which require urgent breast ultrasound to exclude congenital absence of breast tissue
options.rationale: Widely spaced nipples are a dysmorphic feature without imaging indication in the newborn period. Would never be correct (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Low posterior hairline, which warrants urgent thyroid ultrasound for ectopic thyroid
options.rationale: Thyroid disease in Turner is monitored over time via TFTs, not via newborn US based on hairline. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG: Turner Syndrome).

explanation.summary: Turner syndrome (45,X and variants) classically presents at birth with lymphoedema and webbed neck (residual cystic hygroma) or later with short stature and primary ovarian failure. Congenital heart disease occurs in 25 to 45%, predominantly bicuspid aortic valve and coarctation, with aortic dissection risk in adulthood. All confirmed or suspected Turner syndrome patients need echo at diagnosis, with periodic surveillance lifelong.
key_points: 30% of Turner neonates have CHD; BAV and coarctation predominate
Newborn signs: lymphoedema, webbed neck, weak femorals
Later: short stature, delayed puberty, primary ovarian failure
Renal anomalies (horseshoe kidney) in 30%
Lifelong cardiac surveillance for aortic dilatation
explanation.pearls: Any female newborn with unexplained pedal lymphoedema should have femoral pulses checked carefully and a karyotype sent.

stem:
A 3-year-old boy is referred to a community paediatrician for short stature and a heart murmur. He was born at term with a birthweight of 3.2 kg. Height has tracked along the 3rd centile and weight on the 10th centile, despite a mid-parental height on the 50th centile. He sat unsupported at 9 months, walked at 18 months and now uses only 2 to 3 word phrases.

He has hypertelorism with downslanting palpebral fissures, low-set posteriorly rotated ears, a webbed neck with low posterior hairline, and a pectus carinatum superiorly with pectus excavatum inferiorly. There is a 3/6 ejection systolic murmur at the upper left sternal edge that is loudest on inspiration, with a soft single S2 and no thrill. Four-limb blood pressures are symmetric and femoral pulses are equal.

Echocardiography shows a thickened, dysplastic pulmonary valve with a peak gradient of 45 mmHg, mild post-stenotic dilatation of the main pulmonary artery, and no left ventricular hypertrophy.

data_table.Dysmorphology: Hypertelorism with downslanting fissures, low-set posteriorly rotated ears, webbed neck, pectus carinatum superior/excavatum inferior
data_table.Growth: Height 3rd centile, weight 10th centile, mid-parental height 50th centile
data_table.Cardiac: 3/6 ESM ULSE louder on inspiration, soft single S2, no thrill, no LVH, symmetric arm BPs
data_table.Echo: Dysplastic pulmonary valve, peak gradient 45 mmHg, no LV hypertrophy
data_table.Development: Walked 18 months, 2 to 3 word phrases, mild delay

lead_in: Which combination of underlying diagnosis and confirmatory genetic test is most appropriate?

options.text: Williams syndrome; request fluorescence in situ hybridisation for the 7q11.23 elastin region
options.rationale: Williams has supravalvular aortic stenosis with elfin facies; this child has dysplastic pulmonary valve stenosis with Noonan facies. Would be correct with elfin facies and supravalvular AS (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Turner syndrome; request peripheral blood karyotype with 30-cell count for mosaicism
options.rationale: Turner affects phenotypic females with bicuspid aortic valve and coarctation; this patient is male with PS. Would be correct only in a phenotypic female (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Noonan syndrome; request a multi-gene RASopathy panel (PTPN11, SOS1, RAF1 and related genes)
options.rationale: Dysplastic pulmonary valve stenosis (50 percent of cases) with short stature, hypertelorism with downslanting fissures, webbed neck, pectus carinatum/excavatum and mild developmental delay is the Noonan phenotype. PTPN11 mutations are responsible for about half of cases, but several RASopathy genes can produce the phenotype, so a multi-gene panel (not single-gene PTPN11) is the appropriate first-line genetic test per RCH CPG.

options.text: Marfan syndrome; request FBN1 sequencing
options.rationale: Marfan features tall stature, arachnodactyly, ectopia lentis and aortic root dilatation; short stature with webbed neck and PS does not fit. Would be correct with tall stature and lens dislocation (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Marfan).

options.text: Trisomy 21; request peripheral blood karyotype
options.rationale: T21 features upslanting fissures, single palmar crease and AVSD; downslanting fissures and pulmonary valve dysplasia do not fit. Would be correct with classic T21 features (Source: RCH CPG).

explanation.summary: Noonan syndrome is an autosomal dominant RASopathy with variable expression. About 50% have pulmonary valve stenosis (often dysplastic and resistant to balloon valvuloplasty) and 20% have hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. Short stature, characteristic facies (hypertelorism with downslanting fissures, low-set posteriorly rotated ears, webbed neck), pectus deformity, mild intellectual disability, cryptorchidism, and bleeding diathesis are typical. Half the cases are due to PTPN11 mutations.
key_points: PTPN11 in approximately 50%; other RAS-MAPK genes (SOS1, RAF1, KRAS) account for most others
Dysplastic pulmonary stenosis is the cardiac hallmark
HCM in 20%; can present as infant heart failure or sudden cardiac death
Growth hormone is approved for short stature
Echo and ECG at diagnosis and periodically
explanation.pearls: A boy with the Turner phenotype is the classic teaching cue for Noonan syndrome (formerly mis-called male Turner).

stem:
A 14-year-old boy is referred to a paediatric cardiology clinic by his GP because of a tall, thin habitus and a recently identified murmur during a sports pre-participation examination. He has a height on the 99th centile and an arm span 7 cm greater than his height, with a reduced upper-to-lower segment ratio. He has long, slender fingers with positive Steinberg (thumb) and Walker-Murdoch (wrist) signs bilaterally.

There is significant pectus excavatum requiring surgical consideration, a high-arched palate with dental crowding requiring orthodontic intervention, and mild scoliosis with Cobb angle 22 degrees on standing radiograph. An ophthalmology slit-lamp examination performed last month demonstrates bilateral superior ectopia lentis with mild myopia. Echocardiogram performed today shows aortic root dilatation at the sinuses of Valsalva of 4.4 cm, corresponding to a Z-score of 3.8 indexed to body surface area, with mild aortic regurgitation and no evidence of dissection.

The remaining cardiac anatomy is normal. His father died suddenly aged 38 from a presumed dissection of the ascending aorta while playing tennis; no formal post-mortem genetic testing was performed at the time. He currently plays competitive rugby league at school and trains four times per week with intensive resistance work. There is no history of joint dislocation or skin hyperextensibility.

data_table.Skeletal: Height 99th centile, arm span > height by 7 cm, positive thumb (Steinberg) and wrist (Walker-Murdoch) signs, pectus excavatum, scoliosis (Cobb 22 deg), high-arched palate with dental crowding
data_table.Ocular: Bilateral superior ectopia lentis on slit lamp
data_table.Cardiovascular: Aortic root 4.4 cm at sinuses of Valsalva, Z-score 3.8, mild aortic regurgitation
data_table.Family history: Father died at 38 of presumed aortic dissection

lead_in: Which single management plan is most appropriate?

options.text: Reassure as athletic build, continue rugby league, and arrange repeat echo in 5 years
options.rationale: Ghent criteria met (ectopia lentis plus aortic root Z-score over 2 with family history); reassurance and high-impact contact sport is unsafe. Would never be correct (Source: BMJ Best Practice: Marfan).

options.text: Commence sotalol 80 mg twice daily and clear him to play competitive contact sport
options.rationale: Sotalol is not the agent of choice in Marfan; atenolol/propranolol or losartan slow root dilatation, and contact/competitive sport must be avoided. Would never be correct (Source: AHA scientific statement).
source_refs: AHA scientific statement: thoracic aortic disease

options.text: Refer for immediate elective ascending aortic root replacement irrespective of Z-score
options.rationale: Elective root replacement is indicated at >= 5.0 cm in adults, or >= 4.5 cm with family history of dissection/rapid expansion; at 4.4 cm with Z 3.8 medical therapy and surveillance are appropriate. Would be correct at >= 5.0 cm or rapid expansion (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Atenolol or losartan, restrict competitive sport, annual echo and ophthalmology, FBN1 cascade screening
options.rationale: Marfan syndrome with aortic root dilatation requires medical therapy to slow root growth (beta-blocker or losartan, with evidence supporting both), restriction from competitive contact and isometric sport, annual echo surveillance, ophthalmology review for ectopia lentis and retinal complications, and cascade screening of first-degree relatives with FBN1 testing, per BMJ Best Practice and revised Ghent nosology.
source_refs: AHA scientific statement: thoracic aortic disease

options.text: Treat with ramipril 5 mg daily and clear him for non-contact competitive swimming
options.rationale: Losartan (ARB), not ramipril (ACEi), is shown to slow root dilatation; competitive isometric/strenuous sport is restricted. Would be correct only if ramipril and competitive swimming were guideline-endorsed (they are not) (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

explanation.summary: The revised (2010) Ghent nosology diagnoses Marfan syndrome via various combinations of aortic root dilatation, ectopia lentis, FBN1 mutation, family history, and systemic features (systemic score >= 7). This patient meets criteria multiple ways. Management centres on slowing aortic dilatation with beta-blocker or losartan, lifestyle modification, annual echo, ophthalmology review for lens and retinal pathology, and prophylactic root replacement at threshold diameter (commonly >= 5.0 cm, or >= 4.5 cm with adverse features).
key_points: Revised Ghent (2010): aortic root Z >= 2 plus ectopia lentis is sufficient
Beta-blocker (atenolol) or losartan slows root growth
Restrict competitive contact and isometric sport
Annual echo plus ophthalmology
Cascade FBN1 testing of first-degree relatives
explanation.pearls: An aortic root Z-score in childhood is more meaningful than absolute diameter because of body size variability.

sources.label: AHA scientific statement: thoracic aortic disease

stem:
A 7-year-old boy is referred for assessment of moderate intellectual disability (recent WISC FSIQ 58), comorbid attentional difficulties, hand-flapping when excited, poor eye contact, echolalia and tactile defensiveness. He attends mainstream school with full-time aide support. Examination shows a long narrow face with prominent forehead, large protruding ears, a high-arched palate, hyperextensible joints, soft velvety skin, mild pectus excavatum, pes planus, and symmetrically enlarged testes at a Prader orchidometer volume of 6 mL bilaterally for prepubertal age.

There is no microcephaly and no neurocutaneous stigmata. Family history shows a maternal uncle with late-onset intention tremor and gait disorder diagnosed in his 60s, and a maternal grandmother with primary ovarian insufficiency at age 36. Microarray comparative genomic hybridisation at age 4 was normal and a metabolic screen including very long chain fatty acids was normal.

data_table.Cognitive/behaviour: FSIQ 58, global delay, hand-flapping, poor eye contact, echolalia, tactile defensiveness, attentional difficulties
data_table.Examination: Long narrow face, prominent ears, high-arched palate, joint hypermobility, soft velvety skin, mild pectus excavatum, pes planus, prepubertal macroorchidism
data_table.Family history: Maternal uncle late-onset tremor/ataxia diagnosed in 60s; maternal grandmother primary ovarian insufficiency at 36

lead_in: Which diagnosis-and-investigation pair best fits and would have been missed by the prior microarray?

options.text: Klinefelter syndrome; peripheral blood karyotype
options.rationale: Klinefelter has tall stature, small testes (not macroorchidism) and milder cognitive impact; FXTAS/FXPOI family pattern is not explained. Would be correct with 47,XXY phenotype (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Williams syndrome; FISH for 7q11.23 elastin
options.rationale: Williams (7q11.23 deletion) would have shown on prior microarray; elfin facies/supravalvular AS/hypercalcaemia and macroorchidism are inconsistent. Would never be correct given the negative microarray (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Prader-Willi syndrome; DNA methylation analysis at 15q11-q13
options.rationale: Prader-Willi has neonatal hypotonia, hyperphagia, obesity and small testes; macroorchidism does not fit. Would be correct with hypotonia and hypogonadism (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Tuberous sclerosis complex; TSC1 and TSC2 sequencing
options.rationale: TSC features hypopigmented macules, facial angiofibromas, cardiac rhabdomyomas and epilepsy; none present. Would be correct with seizures and skin findings (Source: BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Fragile X syndrome; targeted FMR1 PCR with methylation analysis
options.rationale: Moderate ID with autistic features, long face, prominent ears, joint hypermobility, macroorchidism (already enlarged prepubertally), a maternal uncle with FXTAS and a maternal grandmother with FXPOI fits Fragile X (FMR1 CGG expansion over 200 with methylation). Microarrays do not detect CGG trinucleotide expansions, which is why the prior microarray was normal; FMR1 targeted testing with methylation analysis is the correct next investigation, per RCH CPG: Fragile X.
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice: Fragile X syndrome

explanation.summary: Fragile X syndrome is the commonest inherited cause of intellectual disability and a leading single-gene cause of autism. It results from CGG trinucleotide repeat expansion (> 200) in the 5' untranslated region of FMR1 on Xq27.3, with methylation silencing the gene. Premutation carriers (55 to 200 repeats) can develop FXTAS (older men) or FXPOI (women under 40). Standard microarrays do not detect trinucleotide repeats; targeted FMR1 testing (PCR with methylation analysis or Southern blot) is required.
key_points: FMR1 full expansion > 200 CGG, methylated, silenced
Long face, prominent ears, macroorchidism after puberty, joint hypermobility, autistic features
Premutation 55 to 200: FXTAS in older males, FXPOI in younger females
Microarray does not detect repeat expansions
Cascade testing across maternal line is essential
explanation.pearls: A combination of a boy with intellectual disability, an older male relative with late-onset ataxia/tremor, and a female relative with early menopause is essentially diagnostic of FMR1 pathology until proven otherwise.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice: Fragile X syndrome
